author: @hanslepoeter5167
text: 2 motors for 2 wheels would be a good idea. When you interconnect the wheels RPM is the same for each and small details will cause unevenly devided torque. Certainly in corners. I'v seen people use cheap cordless drills for projects like this. ( hoverboard mainly ). The gearing is just what you need.

author: @mmfc6
text:
I may have missed it in your video, are you just planning on driving one of the rear wheels, or are you incorporating a differential? 
I have a little experience with this having rebuilt the rear end of a kinetic sculpture ( if your curious google The Grand Kinetic Championships in Arcata California) We had been using the differential from a go cart rated to 500 F/Lb's of torque and I rebuilt it into two independent drive systems. (there are two pilots on the machine I raced) When the differential blew out we could not steer at all on pavement (we did fine on sand or gravel). Ackerman steering isn't very complicated, just angle your steering arms in so that when the wheels are turned, a line drawn from either front wheel back toward the rear axle points to the center (centre?) of the rear axle.

author: @wizrom3046
text:
Without a differential it will scrub and chew up the tyres and reduce battery life when cornering.
I think you would have been better with a single wheel drive using the original freewheeling sprocket cluster and a chain from the motor. The freewheeling coasting will improve mileage a LOT, and so will stopping the scrubbing. A solid rear axle is probably the worst configuration you could have chosen.

author: @mrMacGoover
text: I think you forgot why cars have a differential, adult tricycles do not even have both rear wheels locked together on a fixed axle, instead trikes have the chain drive line  only hooked up to one of the rear wheels. the same would apply to a quadracycle .

author: @Gainn
text: The steering being backwards would lead to crash&burn for most people.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
If, when you turn the handle, you are pushing and pulling on the back of the wheels, it will be backwards.
If when you turn the handle, you are pushing and pulling on the front of the wheel, it will turn as expected.

You can set it up to push and pull on the back and have the wheels turn as expected, but you would have to crisscross the linkages and the linkages would have to be much longer. The front setup is much more efficient material wise and no need to give yourself a headache trying to work around all the interferences of the linkages.

replies.author: @Gainn
replies.text: @scotttovey It's not difficult.. You just vertically offset both of the linkages enough that they don't foul and cross them over. There are other ways to do it, but that's the simplest.

replies.author: @scotttovey
replies.text:
@Gainn 
I prefer shorter and less confusing.

replies.author: @mmfc6
replies.text: @realfamilyman I have not found a light weight cheap differential, but if you put a freewheel on both drive wheels and independently drive each wheel, you do not get the scrubbing of a solid drive axle in a turn.
